brief_document.title: Live updates — list rows, detail timeline, resync, firehose
brief_document.purpose: Make the dashboard live: wire the WebSocket subscriptions into the list and detail views so rows and timelines update in real time, with de-duplication, terminal-outcome reflection, drop resync, and a firehose feed.
brief_document.task: Build the live layer on top of AU-004's list and AU-005's timeline: a shared subscription lifecycle hook, a filtered list subscription that patches rows live, a per-workflow subscription that appends events to the timeline (de-duplicated by sequence and reflecting the terminal outcome), resync on socket drop for both views, and a firehose feed for unfiltered observability.

requirements.title: Shared subscription lifecycle and live list rows
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a shared subscription lifecycle hook and use it to drive a filtered list subscription that updates rows live: status transitions update the corresponding row and newly-matching workflows are inserted.
acceptance: src/features/live-feed/hooks/useEventSubscription.ts encapsulates subscribe-on-mount / unsubscribe-on-unmount against the websocket manager for a given filter, exposing the resync signal.

src/features/workflow-list/hooks/useLiveListUpdates.ts subscribes to the active filter and patches the list query cache: a status-changing event updates the matching row; a newly-matching workflow is inserted.
A test feeds synthetic events and asserts a row's status updates and a new matching workflow appears.

requirements.title: Live timeline append with de-dup and terminal outcome
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL subscribe per-workflow on the detail view and append live events to the timeline in sequence order, de-duplicating by sequence number against history already loaded, and reflecting the terminal outcome when the closing lifecycle event arrives.
acceptance: src/features/workflow-detail/hooks/useLiveWorkflowEvents.ts subscribes to the workflow id and merges incoming events into the timeline model in sequence order.
Events already present from the history fetch (same sequence number) are not rendered twice.
When WorkflowCompleted/Failed/Cancelled (or timed-out) arrives, the timeline reflects the terminal outcome and the view stops expecting further events.
A test seeds a history then streams overlapping + new events and asserts no duplicates, correct ordering, and terminal-outcome reflection.

requirements.title: Resync on drop and firehose feed
requirements.spec: WHEN the socket drops, THE SYSTEM SHALL resync the active view — the detail view re-fetches history (or replays after its last-seen sequence) and the list view re-runs its query — with a reconnecting indicator visible. THE SYSTEM SHALL also provide a firehose feed rendering an unfiltered live event stream.
acceptance: On the manager's resync signal, the detail view re-fetches history or replays after_seq and the list view re-runs its query; the ConnectionIndicator shows reconnecting throughout.
FirehoseFeed subscribes to the unfiltered stream and renders incoming events live (scoped by the selected namespace).
A test simulates a drop and asserts the detail view resyncs and the list re-queries; a test asserts the firehose renders streamed events.
The firehose feed renders explicit empty (no events yet) and disconnected states.

boundaries: SHALL NOT reimplement the WebSocket manager or its reconnect — AU-003 (this brief consumes its subscribe/resync surface).
SHALL NOT reimplement the list query or timeline projection — AU-004, AU-005 (this brief patches their caches/models live).
SHALL NOT define the WebSocket protocol or after_seq semantics — that is cluster AW.
verification: bun test passes for the live-update, de-dup, resync, and firehose tests.
Live timeline ordering is strictly by sequence with de-dup; no lint-disable comments added.

checklist.text: A filtered WebSocket subscription updates rows live: status transitions update the corresponding row and newly-matching workflows are inserted.
A per-workflow WebSocket subscription appends live events to the timeline in sequence order as the execution progresses.
Live-appended events are de-duplicated by sequence number against events already loaded from the history fetch.
The timeline reflects the terminal outcome (completed/failed/cancelled/timed-out) when the closing lifecycle event arrives.
On socket drop the detail view resyncs (re-fetch history or replay after last-seen sequence) and the list view re-runs its query, with a reconnecting indicator visible throughout.
A firehose feed renders an unfiltered live event stream for observability.

stories.text: As an on-call operator, I want the workflow list to update live so that status transitions and new workflows appear without me refreshing the page.

As a debugging engineer watching a running workflow, I want new events to stream into the timeline live and the terminal outcome to appear when it completes or fails so that I can watch the execution unfold.
As a platform operator, I want a firehose feed of all events so that I can watch overall engine activity for observability.

constraints.text: Match the apps/web stack exactly: React 19, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind v4, Bun, Biome (100-char), shadcn/Radix primitives, TanStack Query. No new framework, styling system, or state library.

Wire types come from the house generated-types pipeline (Rust #[derive(TS)] → src/types/generated/). The dashboard never hand-defines a type aion-core/aion-proto owns and never hand-edits a generated file.

The dashboard consumes the AW API/WebSocket contract; it defines no server endpoint, no WebSocket protocol, and no asset-hosting mechanism. Not-yet-pinned contract details are isolated in the api module for a one-file change.
Feature-folder convention from apps/web: each feature is components/ + hooks/ (+ context/lib as needed) + types.ts + index.ts. Barrels re-export; logic lives in named files.
Server state goes through TanStack Query; live updates flow through the WebSocket manager and patch query caches or local view state. No bespoke fetch-into-useState for server data.
Every async surface renders explicit loading, empty, and error states. No surface spins indefinitely or blanks on failure.

The WebSocket manager reconnects with bounded backoff on drop and the active view resyncs (re-fetch history/query, or replay after last-seen sequence where supported). A connection indicator is always present; a dropped socket never leaves a silently stale view.
The event timeline is ordered strictly by event sequence number and de-duplicates events arriving via both history fetch and live stream.
Biome lint + format clean (bun run check) and tsc --noEmit clean. No lint-disable comments to silence findings.
Namespace selection scopes every query and every subscription; switching namespace re-scopes both. No unscoped query when a namespace is selected.

resolved_context.intention: Build the human-facing operational web UI for aion-server: a browser application that lets an operator see which workflows are running and which have failed, drill into a single execution's full event history rendered as a timeline, and watch events stream live as they happen. This cluster builds the frontend only. It consumes the HTTP/gRPC list/query/history API and the WebSocket event stream that the server (cluster AW) exposes and hosts as static assets; it does not run the engine, persist anything, or define the API. It must match the existing house frontend stack (React 19 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind v4 + Biome + Bun, shadcn/Radix, TanStack Query, a singleton WebSocket manager with reconnect, generated types from Rust) exactly, so it reads as part of the same codebase as apps/web. When done, an operator pointed at a running aion-server can observe every workflow in every namespace, drill into any execution's full history, and watch events stream in real time — with production-grade loading, empty, error, and reconnection behaviour throughout.

provenance.quote: Build the human-facing operational web UI for aion-server: a browser application that lets an operator see which workflows are running and which have failed, drill into a single execution's full event
